pace and warming and energizing routines will be beneficial to kapha body types now can you realize why you weren't able to perform tadasana like that of your friend when you started yoga this description is only to give you an idea about the shariri kaprakarti and there is much more when there is a combination of body types the concept of prakrti is complex and it is better to consult an ayurveda physician to know more about your prakrti every yogasana and pranayama can be mastered with the correct technique and regular practice what about the ayurvedic concept of diet and lifestyle in yoga practice yoga advices about mitha ahara that is moderate consumption of nutritious food scientific diet is given primary importance in the yoga diet you would be surprised when you know that ayurveda prescribes hitahara mitahara and kalahara for the promotion of health and prevention of diseases wholesome food means moderate consumption in all aspects of food especially the taste the quantity and the varieties and kalahara means the consumption of food at the right time that is after the digestion of the previously ingested meal when you wiki about mitahara you can find the ayurvedic scriptures in the description this shows that diet part of yoga is very well elaborated in ayurveda next the lifestyle in ashtanga are the integral part of yoga they are the first two steps that have been stressed by the yoga challenges as the lifestyle practices these practices are detailed under the dinacharya sadhruta aspects of ayurveda which have to be followed regularly the 10 yamas are almost similar to that of the shavitappa karmas that ayurveda forbids in sadhruta thus ayurveda and yoga go hand in hand if your objectives are physical and mental health if you are looking for atmanyana or spiritual knowledge then ayurveda builds the foundation and helps you master the first four stages of ashtanga yoga starting from yama niyama asana thanks for explaining about the relationship between yoga and ayurveda doctor what is your advice on yoga practice practicing yoga beyond the yogasana and pranayama sessions in every action you do is the real purpose of yoga yoga is the best way to create awareness of you within yourself you become aware of your body parts you become aware of your breathing you become aware of your thoughts and emotions and you become aware of your sensual experiences it is the way to reconnect to yourself i find many people who are enthusiastic about their yoga practices at first and gradually lose their interest finding the yoga routine and lifestyle practices that align with your body nature is one of the keys to pursue yoga without any lack of enthusiasm an ayurveda physician will help you find your nature and the lifestyle to balance your energies the yoga master can guide you with the right practices of yogasana pranayama and meditation is ayurveda service available here in malaysia the government of india has debuted one ayah with a physician and two ayurveda therapists to the traditional and complementary medicine division of ministry of health malaysia four ayurveda treatments shirodhara kathi bhasti griva bhasti and janu bhasti are offered at the 2 tncm units in the malaysian government hospitals namely hospital port diction and hospital rehabilitation chairs you should get a referral letter from a registered medical practitioner either from the government clinic or private clinic to avail these services thanks for this informative program doctor this interaction widened my view on ayurveda and yoga hope you all found this program to be helpful on the seventh international yoga day let us integrate yoga and ayurveda in our lives for health and well-being thank you thanks for watching

text: Yoga for one earth, one health. We all remember the beautiful message of Indian culture, Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam, the whole world, the whole earth is one. It is not just one. All that is visible as variations, all that is visible as diversities is only an apparent, but actually they are all interrelated, interdependent and interconnected. We should remember maintaining health of our body, maintaining health of our mind is not just dependent on ourself. In Bhagavad Gita we have beautiful shlokas. It says that samatvam yoga uchyate, samatvam is not just equanimity, it is balance. So all the actions of thought, all the actions of emotions, all the actions of behaviour and all the actions of physical, of all the living creatures, how beautiful it is, it is interconnected. Let us practise yoga, not just physical asanas, not just breathing exercises, not just emotional exercises. Yoga is not just that. Yoga is transforming our life, transforming our behaviour. June 21, 2025 is approaching. We are all aware 21st of June is the international day of yoga. This year the theme is aptly designed, named and fixed beautifully as the yoga for one earth, one health. We all remember the beautiful message of Indian culture, Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam, that the whole world, the whole earth is one. It is not just one, it is a single unit. This oneness, single unit means all that is visible as variations, all that is visible as diversities is only an apparent, but actually they are all interrelated, interdependent and interconnected. This interconnected, interdependent and interrelated unity of entire life on this globe, on this earth is what is an important dimension. Recognising ourself as part of the entire creation in the globe is an important dimension of yoga that we are not separate, we are connected. We are connected means we are connected to everything. We are connected to the animals, whether they are wild or domestic. We are also connected to all the plants in our own home, surrounding us or even in the forests. We are connected to the entire pancha bhutas, the water, the air, the earth, the fire, the light, the sky. This oneness is a message of yoga and one earth, one health means maintaining the health of all these things. A healthy environment, a healthy planet, a healthy society, a healthy family, a healthy surroundings, the hygiene surrounding us, the hygiene surrounding our home, within the house, surrounding the home, in the streets, in the town, in the village and similarly maintaining, supporting the environment, taking care of the plants, the trees, the leaves, the flowers, the fruits and at the same time supporting the life of the birds, the animals, avoiding violence towards the nature, avoiding violence towards all the living beings. Even our behaviour towards non-living beings, how do we handle, how do we treat the material world, the materials, all this forms part of the health because every behaviour, every action, every thought of ourself not only brings a change in our health, not only brings and affects our health but also brings a change in everything because it is interconnected. This transformative approach, transformative health, transformative affect is an important dimension, we have to recognise that and this recognition makes us to monitor, to alter, to transform, to change our behaviour, our approach, our way of life in the entire world. And this is an important dimension of one earth and one health. We should remember maintaining health of our body, maintaining health of our mind is not just dependent on ourself because it is connected to the entire environment, it is connected to the entire living beings in the world. So if our health or my health is to be maintained means it is dependent on the water I consume, it is dependent on the liquids I consume, it is dependent on the thoughts which enter into me, it is dependent on the emotions which I transact and come to me, it is dependent on the entire environmental safety, it is dependent on the food I eat, that's why healthy food, food safety, healthy environment. I should be part of maintaining and take the responsibility in maintaining the health of my surroundings. So world responds as I respond to it. This inter-responsiveness is an important factor in the yoga dimension. Yoga recognises it. In Bhagavad Gita we have beautiful shlokas. It says that samatvam yoga uchyate. Samatvam is not just equanimity, it is balance. A balanced and harmonious approach and a balanced and harmonious environment, a balanced and harmonious interactions, a balanced and harmonious thought flow, a balanced and harmonious emotions, important dimension. My emotions are affected, my emotions are changed. When my pet dog comes to me, when a pet animal comes to me, how do I handle the animals surrounding me? A cow in my goshala, a dog in our house, a cat in our house, the various insects, the birds. Yogic life recognises and makes a person responsible for that approach. This is an important dimension. Samatvam yoga uchyate means that. And there is another beautiful shloka in Bhagavad Gita, the third chapter, fourteenth shloka in the Karma Yoga. annad bhavanti bhutani parjanyad annasambhavaha yajnyad bhavati parjanyo yajnyakarma samuddhavaha it is said. Entire philosophy of yoga is stitched limit. This is the core of philosophy of yoga. annad bhavanti bhutani. All of us and all the bhutas, entire life is born out of anna. Anna is not just food we eat. Anna is the material in entire world, the cause of our birth. The material from which we are born, the conglomeration and the core and the contents of the material from which we are born, that is anna. annad bhavanti bhutani. So these materials responsible for us, we are born of these materials. Simply we can refer as the food. parjanyad annasambhavaha. The food and these materials are born out of, made out of, prepared out of, generated out of, born out of, manifested out of, parjanyaha, out of rain. See how our life is connected to the food and the food is connected to the rain. parjanyad annasambhavaha and yajnyad bhavanti parjanyaha. And the rains are born or rains are generated out of yajnyaha. Yajnya is result of our actions. Yajnya is a socially responsible action. All our socially responsible behaviour. Yajnya is not just havan. Yajnya is not just homam. Yajnya is a socially responsible and socially productive action of mind. A socially responsible, socially productive thought of mind. A socially responsible, socially productive emotions of mind, thought of mind, actions of mind. Everything which is socially responsible, environmentally responsible, environmentally non-destructive action, environment friendly action, environment friendly thought, that is all called as yajnyaha. And out of this action, out of this thought, rains are generated. Rains are not generated just from the clouds. Rains are generated. It's a cycle of rains. And this cycle of rain has an impact. It has an effect. It has a resultant of my behaviour. parjanyad annasambhavaha and yajnyad is connected with the parjanya. And yajnyad bhavati karmaha it is called. Every action of mind, of all human beings, of all living creatures, all the actions of all the living creatures, and actions are not physical. Actions are emotional. Actions are thought. So all the actions of thought, all the actions of emotions, all the actions of behaviour, and all the actions of physical, of all the living creatures, how beautiful it is. It is interconnected. It is interdependent. And it is interrelated, interdependent, and interconnected it is. So every action of the cow, every action of a small insect, every action of the bird which is flying, is also connected to me, to my thought, to my actions. How wonderful it is. This is the global view, or the global view of yoga, or yogic view of global health. This is the theme of this year's 2025, June 21st, One Earth, One Health. And we should remember beautifully the wonderful yoga protocol which is made, a common yoga protocol. So all the actions we do in the form of yogasanas, all the actions which we perform in the form of pranayama, all the actions which we perform in the name of dhyana, meditation, they all have a global implications. They have global transformations. We should not just say, when I breathe in, and when I breathe out. This action of breathing in and breathe out has a global ramifications. And that is what the beautiful shloka, the third chapters, 14th shloka referred it. So let us not disconnect ourselves from anything or everything from the world and in fact recognise the connectiveness with everything. And this recognition is the first step in maintaining the, not only our health, but the global health. A small insect, a small worm, a small bird, a small snake in the amazon forest will have its thought process connected to my thought sitting somewhere in a small corner of India or somewhere in the world. They are not disconnected, they are interconnectedness. This is called as Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam. That's why the entire world celebrates this year's One Earth and One Health. Let us practise yoga, not just physical asanas, not just breathing exercises, not just emotional exercises. Yoga is not just that. Yoga is transforming our life, transforming our behaviour and bringing a transformation in the world. So my transformation brings a transformation in the world. This essential interconnectedness, interdependence and interrelatedness is the theme of this year's yoga day, International Day of Yoga. And let us practise intensely, spread this message. Let thousands and millions practise yoga and let us be true seekers of yoga, true practitioners of yoga, true messengers of yoga, spreading harmony, peace in this global health scenario. And let us be true yogis and spreading this message of eternal Indian message of science of yoga. And let us be proud to be the forerunners and torchbearers of our ancient message of Bharatiya Sanatana Dharma Samskriti, the Vaidika Upanishad Parampara, which India has presented to the world as a global peace scenarios message. Namaste and thank you one and all.

text: International Day of Yoga is the celebration of India's gift to the world and that is yoga. Observed every year on the 21st of June, this day celebrates the holistic benefits of yoga on our physical, mental and spiritual well-being. Hello and welcome to Dishias. My name is Saloni Nankuly and today we will see why this day is in news. What is the international day of yoga? We will learn a little bit about yoga, the history of yoga and also look at a practice question for prelims. So why is this day in news? Because today is 21st of June and every year on this day we celebrate the international day of yoga worldwide. And why do you think this day was selected? So 21st of June is the longest day of the year. It's the summer solstice and this is something that's known as dakshinyan which means that our sun starts with its southward journey. So during this time of the year people are engaged in spiritual activities in religious activities connecting with their inner self. So that is one of the reasons that 21st of June specifically was selected to be celebrated as the international day of yoga. And the theme for this year is yoga for one earth, one health. And when did all this start? So India is the motherland of yoga. India is where yoga originated from. And the idea to celebrate the international day of yoga was also a brainchild of India. So India gave this idea during the 69th session of the United Nations General Assembly which was held in 2014 and the very same year in 2015 we hosted the first international yoga day celebrations at Rajput in New Delhi and we created two Guiney's world records because this was world's largest yoga session 35,985 people from 84 nationalities participated in this event and because it was celebrated created on such a large scale on such a a grand scale this created two Guiney's world records. Now if you understand what yoga is so yoga does not deal only with the physical fitness of the body. It takes care of your physical, mental, emotional, spiritual well-being and it talks about the union of atma with brah. So the word yoga is derived from the Sanskrit word yuch which means to join or unite and this symbolizes the union of body and consciousness and that is celebrated across the world in various forms because of the advanced benefits that this has and if you look at the history of yoga so yoga is not something that originated in the modern times. It dates back to the ancient times. It dates back to the Harapan Valley civilization 2700 B.CE See we have something which is known as the pashupati seal and this pashupati or the lord of animals is sitting in a yogic posture which is known as mulband assan. So because Pashupati is sitting is believed to be sitting in this yogic posture. We anticipate we speculate that this goes back like yoga goes back to the Harapan Valley civilization and even post Harapan Valley during the Vic times the word yoga was first mentioned in ancient Sanskrit text of Rigveda and it also finds mention inveha you don't have the word yoga mentioned but it talks about the importance of controlling the breath. So rig aur they also talk about it but we cannot exactly pinpoint the exact timeline of this because veeddas are a porche they are not written by humans they were spread by the word of mouth so we cannot pinpoint to the exact era but we find mention in the rigid and atarvid also post the vic era we have pre-classical era which was predominated by opanishads so we have 108ishetts and out of the 108 openish 20 are yogicets. So yogits they deal with the different types of yoga asan pranayam and sadha dhan samadhi things of that sort. So there are 20 yoga opishetss in total and this tells us how important yog has been since ancient times. Post this even in the classical period we see that yoga grew from Hinduism and it entered into Buddhism and Janism. So in Buddhism it was introduced by Goautam Buddha and in Janism intro it was introduced by Lord Mahavir. After Buddhism in Janism we see that Maharishi Patanjali who is considered to be the father of yoga. The yoga is one of the six classical schools of philosophy. So Maharishi Patanjali founded the yog school of philosophy and this he introduced something which is known as Raja yoga. Raja yoga or ashtang yoga. So what do we mean by raja yoga? Just like a king has complete control over his kingdom in raja yoga we focus on selfmastery. We focus on controlling our inner self, our inner emotions through meditation. So raja yoga was introduced by Maharishi Patanjali and ashtanga or also known as eight limbs. It talks about the eight limbs of yoga like pranayam, sadhana, samadhi, nyam, yam all these things it is talked about in the in the ashtang yoga. Then in the postclass period this is the time of bti movement. We see adishankarachara madhavachara ramanujachara miraabai and purand das they are also practicing yogic traditions. So yoga was never lost. It was always there. It has been there since the time immemorial since Harapan civilization then during Vic times also then in Buddhism Janism also and then during Bhi tradition also we see yoga being present in the Indian civilization. Post this in the modern period Swami Viveanand is considered to be one of the most modern yogis who focused on yoga as a manner of being physically strong. And after Maharashi Viveanand we have Ramana Maharashi Ram Krishna Paramhand BK Syaba Joyce a lot of people who practiced yogic tradition. So yoga has always been there and it is just growing and evolving as the time passes by. Now because yoga has been so engraved in the Indian culture in the Indian civilization there have been a couple of initiatives that India has taken in this regard because India wants to establish itself as a global leader in yoga. So it has done some things like the ministry of Aayush in its common yoga protocol has listed yum nam asan all these things are the limbs of ashtang yoga which was introduced by Patanjali Maharshi among the popular yoga sadhas. Then the beauty and wellness sector skill council has also introduced vocational education courses in yoga for CBSC schools. So we are also focusing on encouraging students schoolgoing students to practice yoga for their physical and mental wellness. text: We live in a world that is deeply obsessed with youth. Every single year, global consumers pour billions of dollars into skin care, trendy supplements, and experimental therapies, all chasing the illusion of immortality. We are desperately trying to pause the clock and outrun time. But modern longevity medicine is forcing us to face a beautiful yet complex reality. Humanity is winning the fight to live longer. According to the World Health Organization, by the year 2050, the global population over the age of 60 will double to a staggering 2 billion people. But here is the multi-billion dollar question we must ask ourselves. Are we actually living better or are we just staying alive longer? What good is a long lifespan if it is bound to a wheelchair? What is the true value of longevity if it is defined by chronic joint pain, neurological decline, and a fading grip on your physical independence. This year, the United Nations and the Ministry of Aush have announced the official theme for the 12th International Day of Yoga 2026. Yoga for healthy aging. With major celebrations taking place across the world, from the United Nations headquarters in New York to large-scale gatherings throughout India, the global conversation is undergoing a significant shift. This is no longer simply a symbolic cultural celebration. It is increasingly becoming part of a broader discussion about preventive health care, quality of life, and healthy aging. Today, we're going beyond the slogans. We're going to explore what modern research says about yoga's role in brain health, mobility, stress management, and maintaining independence as we age. Because the most important investment you will ever make is not in your bank account. It is in the future version of yourself. Let's first isolate the brain. In the digital economy and healthcare industry, cognitive longevity has become one of the most valuable areas of research. Why? Because one of the most feared aspects of aging is not physical decline. It is cognitive decline. Memory loss, reduced concentration, difficulty learning new information, and the gradual loss of mental sharpness. For decades, scientists believed many aspects of brain aging were largely unavoidable. Today that understanding is evolving. Researchers increasingly recognize that lifestyle factors such as movement, stress management, sleep quality, and mindfulness may play an important role in supporting long-term cognitive health. This is where yoga becomes particularly interesting. One of yoga's most studied practices is pranayyama or controlled breathing. When you engage in slow, deliberate diaphragmatic breathing, you are doing much more than simply relaxing. You activate the parasympathetic nervous system, the body's natural recovery system. This helps regulate stress responses, supports emotional well-being, and encourages physiological balance. Why does this matter? Because chronic stress influences nearly every system in the body. Poor sleep, elevated cortisol, cardiovascular strain, mental fatigue, and reduced overall well-being. Every time you consciously slow your breathing, you are creating conditions that support healthier aging. You aren't simply calming your mind. You are investing in one of your most valuable assets, your brain. As the legendary yoga master BKSar beautifully stated, yoga cultivates the ways of maintaining a balanced attitude in day-to-day life and endows skill in the performance of one's actions. Now, let us pivot to the physical body. The single greatest threat to an aging adult's independence is not necessarily a lack of stamina. It is loss of mobility, loss of balance, and the increased risk of falls. The World Health Organization has repeatedly identified physical inactivity as a major risk factor for poor health outcomes worldwide. As we age, maintaining movement becomes increasingly important. When an individual steps onto a yoga mat and stands in tatasana or mountain pose, they are engaging a complex network of muscles and balance systems. When they move into trionosana or triangle pose, they challenge flexibility, stability, and body awareness simultaneously. These movements stimulate proprioception, the body's ability to sense position and movement. Strong proprioception supports balance. Strong balance supports independence and independence is one of the greatest predictors of quality of life in older adulthood. Yoga also offers something many people desperately need. Accessible movement. Millions of adults avoid exercise because they believe they are too stiff, too old, too inexperienced or too fragile. Yoga provides a low impact alternative. Through controlled movement and gradual progression, practitioners can improve flexibility, mobility, and confidence without excessive strain. The goal isn't becoming extraordinarily flexible. The goal is maintaining the ability to move freely throughout daily life, to bend, to reach, to climb stairs, to travel, to play with grandchildren, and to continue living independently for as long as possible. Another challenge that accelerates aging is chronic stress. Modern life keeps many people in a near constant state of mental stimulation. Emails, deadlines, notifications, financial pressures, family responsibilities. The body was never designed to remain under continuous stress. Yet for millions of people, stress has become the default setting. Yoga provides a structured opportunity to interrupt that cycle through mindful movement, breath awareness, and focused attention. Practitioners create space for recovery. Better recovery often leads to better sleep. Better sleep supports physical restoration and physical restoration supports healthy aging. This is one reason yoga is increasingly practiced not only by retirees but also by professionals, executives, healthare workers, entrepreneurs, and athletes. It is not merely a fitness practice. It is a recovery practice. Because of these far-reaching benefits, yoga is increasingly being recognized as a practical tool within preventive health and wellness strategies. The goal is no longer to practice yoga once a year on International Yoga Day. The goal is to integrate it into daily life. If you would like to participate in this global movement, several initiatives make getting started easier than ever. The Yoga Sangam platform allows individuals, schools, workplaces, and community organizations to participate in coordinated yoga day activities. Educational resources, guided sessions, and beginnerfriendly learning materials continue to expand through government and wellness platforms. The message is simple. You do not need expensive equipment. You do not need years of experience. You do not need exceptional flexibility. You only need a willingness to begin because every expert was once a beginner. And every healthy habit starts with a single step or perhaps a single breath. Healthy aging is not about avoiding the passage of time. It is about improving the quality of the years we have. Whether you are currently in your 20s building a foundation for the future, in your 40s balancing work and family responsibilities, or in your 70s striving to maintain mobility and independence, the principles remain the same. Move regularly, manage stress, sleep well, stay mentally engaged, and invest consistently in your future self. To the younger generation watching, do not wait until health becomes a problem before making health a priority. The habits you build today become the reality you experience tomorrow. And to older viewers, your best years are not defined by your age. They are defined by your willingness to remain active, engaged, curious, and connected to life. Your families need your wisdom. Your communities need your experience. And the world benefits from your continued presence. One day, every single one of us will grow older. That part is inevitable. But how we experience those years is influenced by the choices we make today. The goal is not merely to extend lifespan. The goal is to extend health span to remain mentally sharp, physically capable, emotionally resilient, and fully engaged with life. That is the true meaning of healthy aging. And that is why the world is celebrating the 12th International Day of Yoga 2026 under the theme yoga for healthy aging. If you found value in this science-backed exploration of longevity and wellness, please hit the like button, subscribe for more evidence-based health content, and share this video with someone you care about, and tell us in the comments, what is one habit you're starting today to improve your future health span. Thank you deeply for your time and attention. Namaskar.
title: International Yoga Day 2026 | Yoga for Healthy Ageing | Can Yoga Slow Aging?

Yoga is an invaluable gift of ancient Indian tradition. It embodies unity of mind and body, thought and action, restraint and fulfillment, harmony between man and nature, and a holistic approach to health and well-being. Being yoga is not about exercise but to discover the sense of oneness with ourselves, the world and nature. By changing our lifestyle and creating consciousness, it can help us to deal with the climate change. As you all are aware on December 11, 2014, the 193 member UNGA approved the proposal by consensus with a record of 177 co-sponsoring countries a resolution to establish 21st June as international day of yoga. In its resolution, the UNGA recognized that yoga provides a holistic information about the benefits of practicing yoga for the health of the world population. Yoga also brings harmony in all walks of life and thus is known as disease prevention, health promotion and management of many lifestyle related disorders. If anybody asks what is yoga, yoga is essentially a spiritual discipline based on an extremely subtle science which focuses on bringing harmony between mind and body. It's an art and science for healthy living. The word yoga is derived from the Sanskrit root yuj meaning to join to unite. According to yogic scriptures, the practice of yoga leads to the union of individual consciousness with universal consciousness. According to modern scientists, everything in the universe is just a manifestation of the same quantum firmament. One who experiences this oneness of existence is said to be in yoga. And the individual can be termed as an yogi who has attained the state of freedom referred to as mti, nirvana, kywalia or moka. Yoga also refers to an inner science comprising of a variety of methods through which human beings can achieve union between the body and mind to attain selfreization. The aim of yoga practice is to overcome all kinds of sufferings that lead to a sense of freedom in every walk of life with holistic health, happiness and harmony. If we look into uh the history or the development of yoga, the science of yoga has its origin thousands of years ago. Yoga is widely considered as an immortal cultural outcome of the Indis Saraspati Valley civilization dating back to 2,700 BC and has proven itself to cater to both material and spiritual uplift of humanity. The presence of yoga is also available in folk traditions, Vic and Upanishad heritage, Buddhist and Jane traditions, Danas, epics of Bahabarata including Bhagavat Gita and Ramayana. Though yoga was being practiced in the pre-wdic period, the great sage Maharshi Patanjali systematized and codified the then existing yogic practices, its meaning and its related knowledge through Patanjali yoga sutras. Yoga has spread all over the world by the teachings of eminent yoga masters from ancient times to present date. Today everybody has conviction about yoga practices towards the prevention of disease, maintenance and promotion of health. Millions and millions of people across the globe have benefited by the practice of yoga. And the practice of yoga is blossoming and growing more vibrant with each passing day. The fundamentals of yoga is as follows. Yoga works on the level of one's body, mind, emotion and energy. This has given rise to four broad classifications of yoga. Karma yoga where we utilize the body Ghana yoga where we utilize the mind. Bi yoga where we utilize the emotions and kria yoga where we utilize the energy. Every individual is a unique combination of these four factors. Only a guru, a teacher can advocate the appropriate combination of the four fundamental paths. Gurushisha parumparas of yoga led to the emergence of different traditional schools. These include ghana yoga, bi yoga, karma yoga, patanjali yoga, kundalini yoga, hata yoga, dana yoga, mantra yoga, lia yoga, raja yoga, jane yoga, buddhi yoga and so on. Each school has its own approach and practices that lead to the ultimate aim and objectives of yoga. The widely practiced yoga sadas are yama, nyama, asana, pranayyama, pratyahhara, dharana, dana, samadhi, bandhas and mudras, shhat karmmas, yuktahara, mantra japa, yuk with the karma etc. Yamas are restraints and nyamas are observances. These are considered to be the prerequisites for further yogic practices. asas capable of bringing about stability of body and mind. Pranayama consists of developing awareness of one's breathing followed by willful regulation of respiration as the functional or vital basis of one's existence. It helps in developing awareness of one's mind and helps to establish control over the mind. Pratyahhara indicates dissociation of one's consciousness from the sense organs. That is withdrawal method. which connect with the external objects. Dharana indicates broadbased field of attention that is inside body and mind. Dana is contemplation that is focused attention inside the body and mind. and samadhi it's integration or liberation. How yoga can help? Yoga is essentially a path to liberation from all bondage. However, medical research in recent years have uncovered many physical and mental benefits that yoga offers. I saw a a a small sampling of research shows that yoga is bene beneficial for physical fitness, muscularkeeletal functioning and cardiovascular health. It is beneficial in the management of diabetes, respiratory disorders, hypertension, hypotension and many lifestyle related disorders. Yoga helps to reduce depression, fatigue, anxiety disorders and stress. Yoga regulates many partial symptoms. In essence, yoga is a process of creating a body and mind that are stepping stones, not hurdles to an exuberant and fulfilling life. [Music] This year we are celebrating the international day of yoga with the theme one earth one health. Aligning yoga with sustainability and global well-being. This year is the 11th anniversary of global celebration. June 21st represents a symbolic harmony between nature and human wellness and is important in many cultures. The theme yoga for one earth, one health echoes a vital truth about the interconnectedness of health, sustainability and the environment aligning with India's one Earth, one family, one future, which is the vision highlighted during its G20 presidency. Over the past decades, International Day of Yoga has evolved into a transformative force encouraging holistic health, mindfulness, and harmony across cultures and communities. The International Day of Yoga 2025 is not just a one-day observance. It reflects India's enduring commitment to holistic health, environmental harmony and global well-being. With yoga for one earth, one health as its gratitude principle, India continues to lead the world in connecting physical fitness with mindful living. text: [Music] Welcome to this special video about International Yoga Day 2025, celebrated worldwide every year on June 21st. This date was chosen for its deep symbolic meaning, the summer solstice, the longest day of the year in the northern hemisphere. It's a moment when the sun reaches its highest point, flooding the earth with the most light and energy it will receive all year. Yoga day is more than a celebration of physical exercise. It's a reminder of the ancient practic's power to connect body, mind, and spirit. Not only within ourselves, but with the whole planet. This year's theme, yoga for one earth, one health, captures this beautifully. It urges us to realize that personal health and the health of our environment are inseparable. In this video, we will explore why yoga day is so significant, the science that backs yoga's benefits, the spiritual meaning behind the solstice, and how this ancient practice can help us build a healthier planet and a healthier future. You might wonder, why is International Yoga Day set specifically on June 21st? The answer lies deep in the connection between yoga and the natural rhythms of the cosmos. June 21st marks the summer solstice, the longest day and shortest night in the northern hemisphere. For thousands of years, ancient civilizations across the world, from India to Egypt to the Americas, observe this day as sacred. It symbolized the triumph of light over darkness, warmth over cold, and life's eternal renewal. In yogic tradition, this period coincides with the time called Brahma Mhorta, literally the creator's hour. This is about 1.5 hours before sunrise when the environment is calm, the air is fresh, and the mind is naturally more peaceful and receptive. It's believed to be the best time to meditate, chant, or perform spiritual practices. Scientific studies now confirm what yogis have known for centuries, that early morning practice can optimize brain function, balance hormones, and boost mood. Our circadian rhythms, the body's internal clock, are naturally aligned with these early hours, making it easier to enter a calm, focused state. Choosing June 21st for Yoga Day is therefore a brilliant blend of science, tradition, and symbolism. A perfect day to honor the union of body, mind, and universe. The theme for International Yoga Day 2025 is particularly timely and meaningful. Yoga for one earth, one health. What does this mean? The phrase emphasizes the interconnectedness of human health and the health of our planet. We cannot truly be healthy if the earth's ecosystems are damaged, polluted, or depleted. Similarly, the earth's well-being depends on the choices we make daily, what we consume, how we treat nature, and how mindful we are of our environmental impact. Yoga is more than physical postures. It's a holistic lifestyle rooted in our himsa. Nonviolence and compassion extended not only to ourselves and others but to all living beings and the planet. Practicing yoga awakens a deep awareness of balance between strength and flexibility, effort and surrender, activity and rest. This balance mirrors the balance needed in nature, healthy soils, clean air and water, thriving forests and diverse wildlife. In a world facing rising pollution, climate change, and biodiversity loss, yoga reminds us to slow down, observe, and respect the delicate web of life that supports us all. Through yoga, we learn mindfulness, to live with intention, reduce waste, eat consciously, and nurture both body and earth. The theme calls on us to embody these values personally and collectively, fostering a healthier world. Modern science increasingly validates the benefits of yoga for physical, mental, and emotional health. Harvard Medical School and the World Health Organization have published numerous studies showing yoga's positive effects on the body. Yoga helps lower stress hormones like cortisol, which are linked to heart disease, inflammation, and mental health issues. It lowers blood pressure, improves circulation, and supports the immune system. Beyond the physical, yoga is a powerful tool for mental well-being. In a world where anxiety, depression, and stress related disorders are rising, yoga offers techniques to calm the nervous system, improve mood, and increase resilience, breathing exercises or pranayyama influence the parasympathetic nervous system. The rest and digest mode, reducing feelings of panic and tension. Recent research shows yoga can improve lung capacity and respiratory function, which remains critically important as respiratory illnesses continue to affect millions globally. In 2025, many are still recovering physically and mentally from the CO 19 pandemic. Yoga's integrative approach provides accessible healing and empowerment. The ancient practice of yoga is embracing modern technology in exciting new ways. AI powered virtual yoga instructors are emerging, providing personalized classes tailored to individual needs, preferences, and health conditions. Virtual reality yoga experiences are being developed to immerse practitioners in serene natural landscapes, enhancing focus and relaxation. These innovations make yoga more accessible worldwide, breaking barriers of location, time, and cost. However, no matter how advanced the technology becomes, the essence of yoga remains unchanged. The union of mind, body, and spirit. Technology is simply a tool to help more people discover the transformative power of this timeless practice. Yoga day carries deep spiritual meaning. In India and many parts of the world, the solstice night is seen as a sacred time of renewal and cosmic energy. Millions gather near rivers, temples, and mountains to meditate, chant, and perform Surya Namaskar or sun salutations, a yogic practice honoring the sun, the source of all life and energy. Ancient texts describe this night as charged with prana or life force making spiritual practices more potent. The alignment of sun and earth creates a subtle energy field that yogis say enhances meditation and inner awakening. Many believe that observing these traditions on yoga day can catalyze a collective shift in consciousness, fostering peace, harmony, and healing not only within individuals but throughout the planet. You don't need to be an expert yogi to participate in International Yoga Day. Simple acts like mindful breathing, gentle stretching, or spending time in nature at sunrise can honor the day's spirit. Reducing plastic use, supporting eco-friendly choices, and spreading awareness about environmental conservation align with the theme, one earth, one health. Join local or online yoga events to connect with others and share positive energy. Remember, small mindful steps ripple outwards, creating waves of change that benefit all living beings. This International Yoga Day, let us unite in breath, movement, and purpose to nurture ourselves, each other, and our precious Earth. Thank you for watching. If you found this video inspiring, please like, share, and subscribe for more content exploring the mysteries and wisdom of our world. [Music]
title: International Yoga Day 2025 | Theme : Yoga for One Earth, One Health

text: [Music] Nearly five lakh people participated in a synchronized mass yoga demonstration in Vishaka Patnam with Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi himself leading the charge. Officials designated 326 sections, each hosting about a thousand participants with a fleet of over 3,000 buses mobilized to bring yoga practitioners to the site. The grand spectacle was aimed for multiple record setting feats including one that's recognized by the Guiney's World Records. The security at the site was ramped up with 10,000 police personnel, 1200 CCTV cameras and drone surveillance to ensure safety and precision. PM Modi said that yoga is the way of life and teaches us self-care and discipline. He added that yoga leads us from the journey of me to we initially we learn to take good care of our own health and wellness. Gradually our care and concern extend to our environment, society and planet. Yoga is a great personal discipline. At the same time, it is a system that takes us from me to we. Chief Minister and Chandra Babu Naidu said that the Andhra Pradesh is planning to introduce yoga in the school curriculum from class 9th and even establish a deemed yoga university. Scenes of yoga harmony are also emerging from across the country. At Pangong Soul Lake in Ladak, ITB soldiers from the 24th battalion performed yoga against the pristine himalian backdrop at high altitude border outpost like Dhan Singh tab and charts in Vishaka Patnam and along Tamil Ladu's coast. Indian Coast Guard personnel and families joined in yoga from sea to shore with ICG ship Shya leading yoga at sea and Rani Baka embracing the discipline on sacred shores. And for more on this, our correspondent Nikita Singh has sent us this crown report from New Delhi. Take a look. Mai, tell us about your uh personal yoga journey. text: International Day of Yoga 21st June 2025 theme yoga for one earth one health. Namaste and welcome. Today we gather in spirit and awareness to observe the 11th international day of yoga celebrated on 21st June 2025. This year's theme yoga for one earth one health is more than a message. It is a mission. It highlights the deep connection between individual well-being and planetary health. Yoga, an ancient Indian discipline, is much more than physical exercise. It is a union of body and mind, self and environment, individual and universal consciousness. The word comes from the Sanskrit root yuj which means to join or to unite. It symbolizes harmony, balance and wholeness. Originating thousands of years ago in India, yoga is a spiritual, mental and physical practice. It has stood the test of time and is now practiced across the world in many forms from traditional ha and ashtanga to modern adaptations. Yoga calms the mind, strengthens the body, and fosters mindfulness, enabling us to make healthier and more sustainable lifestyle choices. On 11th December 2014, the United Nations General Assembly adopted a resolution co-sponsored by a record 175 member states declaring 21st June as the International Day of Yoga. This global recognition was made possible by the visionary leadership of Hanlay Prime Minister Shri Narendra Modi who introduced the idea during his address to the 69th session of the UN General Assembly. He said, and I quote, "Yoga is an invaluable gift from our ancient tradition. It embodies unity of mind and body, thought and action, a holistic approach that is valuable to our health and well-being. Since then, every year, millions across the globe come together on this day to celebrate the transformative power of yoga. Yoga is not just an exercise routine. It is a lifestyle, a philosophy, and a way of life that encourages peace, resilience, and self-awareness. This year's theme, yoga for one earth, one health, reflects the Indian philosophy of Vasua Kutumba, meaning the world is one family. It reminds us that when we care for ourselves, we also begin to care for the planet. Mindful living promotes not just personal wellness, but also environmental responsibility. The World Health Organization also recognizes yoga as a key tool in preventing non-communicable diseases such as heart conditions, diabetes, and obesity. All linked to inactivity and poor lifestyle. Yoga is a preventive, sustainable, and accessible solution for global health challenges. As the great yogi BKS Yenar once said, "Yoga cultivates the ways of maintaining a balanced attitude in day-to-day life and endows skill in the performance of one's actions. This balance is what the world urgently needs today. Centuries ago, the poet Bartari beautifully summarized the power of yoga. By practicing yoga, a person gains courage like a father, forgiveness like a mother, mental peace as a true friend, truth as their child, compassion as a sister, self-restraint as a brother, the earth as a bed, and knowledge to satisfy their hunger. Isn't that the ultimate vision of harmony? As we celebrate this 11th International Day of Yoga, let's remember yoga is not about bending our bodies, but about bending our egos, opening our hearts, and uniting with all that surrounds us. Let us commit to a healthier self and in doing so to a healthier earth. Let us celebrate yoga for one earth, one health. Happy International Day of Yoga, 21st June 2025. Join the movement. Breathe, stretch, connect. # yoga 41 earth one health # international yoga
title: International Day of Yoga 2025, Yoga for One Earth, One Health, History, Theme & Global Significance

text: [Music] From sacred silence to billiondoll bus, yoga has stretched far beyond the mad. Welcome to the world where ancient wisdom meets modern wealth. It was Prime Minister Narendra Modi who first proposed the idea of an international day of yoga at the United Nations in 2014. His call to recognize yoga's in universal appeal led to the UN declaring 21st of June as international yoga day. I want to recognize Prime Minister Modi for all he has done to promote understanding of yoga and its many benefits. On this international day of yoga, let us embrace the spirit of unity and resolve to build a better, more harmonious world for people, planet, and ourselves. an unprecedented diplomatic success that turned India's ancient practice into a global movement. Since then, Modi has personally led yoga sessions across iconic locations from New Delhi to New York, bringing together the millions in synchronized practice. His leadership has transformed yoga from a culture tradition into a symbol of global wellness diplomacy. In India, yoga is more than a practice. It's a way of life. But today, it's also a thriving industry. Through initiatives like yoga sung and yoga bundan, India's building global partnership and inclusive communities around yoga. Modi's vision has not only streamed yoga but also positioned India as the epicenter of booming wellness economy. According to the future market insights, India's yoga and meditation service sector is projected to grow from $81.7 billion in 2025 to $155.2 billion by 2035, registering a 6.6% of CAGR. Now luxury wellness retreats are booming offering curated experience that blend yoga ayurveda and mindfulness. These retreats attract the wellness tourists from around the world turning the spiritual travel into a multi-million dollar business. And it's not just about travel. Research scholars predict that the global yoga and heritage industry is already worth 30 to40 billion with projections reaching 70 to $80 billion in the near future. For example, yoga wear has become a fashion statement. From breathable fabrics to eco-conscious design, yoga apparel is now a billions of dollar global market already and it's growing. Brands are capitalizing on the lifestyle shift offering everything from leggings to luxury mats. Under Modi's leadership, yoga has leaped from ancient ash into the digital age. Powered by apps, AI and global liveream, he has imagined yoga not just as a tradition but as tech enabled wellness for the 21st century. Technology is reshaping how we experience yoga. Apps now offer guided session, personalized routines and even AI powered posture correction. Startups are building digital wellness ecosystem making yoga accessible to anyone anywhere from a beach in Goa to a studio apartment in Berlin. [Applause] On yoga day 2025, Modi will lead a historic yoga session in Vishaka Patnam with over five lakh participants under the theme yoga for one earth, one health. The campaign spans over thousands of location nationwide making it one of the largest synchronized yoga demonstrations in the history. Yoga began as a path to inner peace. Today it's also a gateway to global prosperity. India's ancient gift to the world is now shaping the future of wellness, technology and entrepreneurship. This is more than a trend. This is the yoga economy and it's just getting started. [Music] For more such updates, keep watching money control.com and please like, share and subscribe to Money Control's YouTube channel.
title: International Yoga Day 2025: The Rise of the Yoga Economy

text: Hey there, beautiful soul. Welcome to a new day of your yoga journey. I am your yoga partner. Yes, you. The one wondering if yoga is for you. Spoiler alert, it is. And today we're celebrating something so special. International Yoga Day 2025. And this year's theme, yoga for one earth, one health. Sounds beautiful, right? But what does it really mean? Let's start here. What is yoga? Yoga isn't just stretching. It's not just handstands or expensive leggings or fancy poses on a beach. Yoga at its core is about connection. The word yoga literally means union. the union of your body, breath, mind, and the world around you. So when we talk about one earth, one health, we're talking about a deeper connection, not just within ourselves, but with nature, with the planet, with everything we're a part of. One earth, one breath. Here's something yoga has taught me, something that completely changed my life. The way we treat ourselves is often how we treat the world. When we rush, ignore our needs, disconnect from our bodies, we do the same with the planet. We stop noticing the trees, the skies, the soil that supports us. But the opposite is also true. When we slow down, breathe with intention, listen to our bodies, we start to listen to the world again. We eat better, live better, consume less, love more. Healing begins inside and then flows outward. Why yoga is for you, even if you're just starting. Now, I know what you might be thinking. But I'm not flexible. I've never done yoga. I'm too busy. Listen, I've been there. When I first stepped onto a mat, I was stiff, scattered, and super self-conscious. But yoga met me exactly where I was and it will meet you there too. You don't need to be perfect. You just need to be present. Even 5 minutes a day sitting and breathing. That's yoga. And trust me, that's enough to begin shifting your world. A practice that grows with you. Yoga isn't a one-sizefits-all thing. There are gentle flows, restorative practices, deep breath work, mindful movements. And on this channel, we're going to explore all of it slowly, joyfully, and in a way that supports your health and the planets. You'll learn beginner poses for any body, breath work to calm your mind, simple ways to practice yoga at home, no mat or gear required, and the mindset tools that helped me find peace off the mat, too. Because yoga isn't just about moving your body. It's about coming home to yourself and living in harmony with everything around you. Let's grow together. So, here's my invitation to you. Let this International Yoga Day be the day you begin. Not because it's trendy, not because it's perfect, but because something inside you is ready, ready to breathe, to stretch, to grow. You are nature. Your breath is a part of the earth's rhythm. Your healing matters and I'll be right here sharing everything I've learned on my journey to walk beside you on yours. If you're ready to begin, hit that subscribe button and come practice with me. There's a whole world waiting for you inside this breath. One earth, one health, and it all starts with one breath. Namaste.
title: Yoga for One Earth, One Health | What Yoga Really Means in 2025

text: Introduction to World Yoga Day. World Yoga Day also known as International Day of Yoga is celebrated every year on June 21. The day was officially declared by the United Nations in December 2014 following a proposal by Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi. The first celebration took place in 2015 and it has since become a global event promoting the physical, mental and spiritual benefits of yoga. Why June 21? June 21 is the summer solstice, the longest day of the year in northern hemisphere. In yogi tradition, this day holds special significance as it is believed to be the day when Adi Yogi, the first yogi, began sharing his knowledge of yoga with humanity. The 11th International Yoga Day on June 21, 2025 aims to highlight unity in health, both human and planetary, as its core message. Whether you are practicing at home, joining a group event or taking part in community initiatives, the idea is to connect your yoga with a broader context of caring our planet. Theme 2025. The official theme for International Yoga Day 2025 is yoga for one earth, one health. This theme highlights the interconnectedness between human health and the health of the planet. It emphasizes that taking care of our environment directly supports our own well-being and vice versa. The message encourages individuals and communities to embrace yoga not only for personal wellness but also as a practice that nurtures collective and ecological harmony. Why this theme? [Music] The theme for International Yoga Day 2025, yoga for one earth, one health, carries deep significance, especially in the context of today's global challenges. Here is why this theme was chosen. Growing environmental concerns, climate change, pollution, deforestation, and loss of biodiversity are affecting the planet's health and in turn human health. The theme links yoga with ecological consciousness, encouraging people to live in harmony with nature. Post-pandemic awareness. After the coid9 pandemic, there is a stronger global realization of the interconnectedness between human health and planetary health. Clean air, clean water, and a healthy environment are no longer luxuries. They are essentials for survival. Sustainable living through yoga. Yoga promotes values like discipline, simplicity, inner peace and balance which align with the principles of sustainable living. The theme suggests that a yogic lifestyle naturally leads to less consumption, more mindfulness and a lower ecological footprint. Global unity and shared responsibility. The phrase one earth, one health underscores our shared responsibility. Just as yoga connects body, mind and soul, this theme calls for a unified approach connecting people, communities and nations to work together for the well-being of all. [Music] Significance of the theme. Holistic health. It expands the idea of health beyond the individual to include community well-being and environmental sustainability. Yoga as a bridge. It positions yoga as more than a physical exercise, a philosophy and practice that fosters inner and outer balance. Call to action. It invites people to practice yoga not just on the mat but in their choices how they eat, travel, consume and interact with the world. In essence, yoga for one earth, one health reflects the evolving role of yoga in the 21st century. A tool not just for personal transformation but also for planetary healing and collective harmony. [Music]
title: International Day of Yoga 2025 || Yoga for One Earth One Health

text: India is officially stepping onto the mat as the countdown begins to one of the world's biggest wellness movements. The Ministry of Aush is marketing the 100 day countdown to the International Day of Yoga 2026 with a special yoga day countdown which will be held at Vijay Vigan Bhavan in New Delhi on Friday. The Maji Desai National Institute of Yoga Center is hosting the event which signals the start of a nationwide preparations for the 12th edition of the global celebration of yoga. The program will bring together yoga gurus, health experts, policy makers and enthusiasts from across the country. It will be attended by Union Minister of State for Aayush Prattab Prao Jadav, Aayush Secretary Vya Rajesh Kotcha and noted yoga expert HR Nagendra among other dignitaries. The countdown campaign will see 100 organization promoting yoga in 100 cities and locations across India expanding awareness and encouraging more people to make yoga part of their daily lives. The global observance of international yoga day will be celebrated every year on June 21 tracing its origins to a landmark proposal by Prime Minister Narendra Modi that was adapted by the United Nations General Assembly in 2014. Since then, the day had grown into a worldwide movement highlighting yoga as a path to physical health, mental balance, and holistic well-being. text: [Music] On June 21st, the The world once again came together for a powerful celebration. The 11th International Day of Yoga. From the majestic Himalayas to the shores of the Indian Ocean. From bustling cities to quiet villages, millions united in breath, movement, and mindfulness. This year's theme, yoga for one earth, one health, carried a message deeper than ever before. A reminder that our personal well-being is inseparable from the health of our planet. At the very heart of the celebrations stood India, the birthplace of yoga and the spirit of this global movement in Visa Capatnam, Andra Pradesh. An extraordinary event unfolded. Over 300,000 participants gathered in a historic yoga session with Prime Minister Narendra Modi himself. This remarkable gathering set a new world record, doubling previous participation and sending a message of unity through health. The prime minister shared a powerful truth. Yoga is a timeless gift that transcends all boundaries, uniting people in health and harmony. A practice for everyone beyond ability or background. are one hill. [Music] Yoga is not just about the self. It's about the world we share. This year's theme is a call to action to live in balance with the earth. to care for our rivers, our forests, our air. In yoga, we practice awareness and that awareness extends to how we treat our environment. The world is one family. Yoga teaches us not only to breathe mindfully, but to live mindfully. Across the ocean, the global spirit of yoga was on full display. At the United Nations headquarters in New York City, the permanent mission of India hosted a special guided session led by Dr. Deepac Chopra drawing over Kan 200 participants from diplomats to yoga enthusiasts. Meanwhile, in the heart of Manhattan, Time Square became a sea of serenity during the annual Solstice in Time Square, mind over madness yoga. Thousands gathered in this iconic crossroads, including acclaimed actor Anupam Kair, turning one of the world's busiest places into a sanctuary of stillness. A moment that truly captured yoga's universal power to calm, to connect, and to inspire. Three, four, and five. Inhale. Rise up. Exhale. Here in the United States, yoga has become more than a practice. It's a daily rhythm of life. From city parks to meditation centers, from Los Angeles to Pennsylvania, yoga is embraced by people of all ages and backgrounds. Take the Pennsylvania meditation center for example, where yogis gathered on this day to honor tradition and deepen their spiritual connection through yoga and meditation. This day is not just an event. It's a movement that lives within us. Yoga reminds us that we are all connected to the breath, to the earth, and to each other. This International Yoga Day, we celebrate not only a tradition, but a way of life that offers healing, balance, and unity. Yoga for one earth, one health. A message for today, a legacy for tomorrow. text: Hello everyone, hope all are doing well. In today's session, we are going to discuss about international day for yoga which is to be celebrated on 21st of June every year. And this year in 2025, it marks the 11th anniversary of this global celebration. Every year this date celebration has a different theme and this year it is to be yoga for one earth one health. This theme emphasizes the interconnections of individual well-being and the health of our planet. Thus highlighting how yoga can contribute to both. It was announced by Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi in his monkey bath radio broadcast. Now let's see its history and significance. The idea of celebration was proposed by Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi during its speech at UN General Assembly in the year 2014 September recognizing the universal appeal and holistic benefits of yoga. The United Nations General Assembly officially proclaimed June 21st as the International Day of Yoga on December 11, 2014 with a record of 177 member co-states co-sponsoring the resolution and the first day of yoga was celebrated on 21st June 2015 and it has become now a global moment. Now let's try to find out why exactly on 21st of June. This date was chosen because it is the summer solstice in northern hemisphere the longest day of the year. It holds special significance in many parts of the world and it's seen as a time of spiritual awakening and renewal making it an ideal day to celebrate the practice of yoga which aims to harmonize the body, mind and spirit once. Now let's try to find out what exactly is meant by yoga. The word yoga is derived from Sanskrit and means to join or to unite symbolizing the union of body and consciousness or individual consciousness with the universal consciousness. Now let's see its benefits. The celebration aims to raise awareness worldwide about the numerous benefits of practicing yoga which include improved physical health, flexibility, strength, balance, posture, mental well-being like reduce stress, anxiety, depression, improved clarity and focus as well spiritual growth and a sense of inner peace. This International Yoga Day serves as a powerful reminder of yoga's ability to unite people across cultures and backgrounds in a shared pursuit of health, harmony, and peace. Wishing everyone a very happy international yoga and expecting everyone to include it in their schedule. Thank you.

The active participation of the Ministry of Civil Aviation reflects our collective commitment to personal, environmental and global well-being. text: [Music] Hello, I am Dr. Kuml Prasad, clinical lead and senior consultant neurosurgeon at Narana Health. June 21st, we celebrate International Day of Yoga. Yoga is an ancient tradition which has roots in India which gives a solution for universal peace, harmony and to bring in personal physical well-being. both physical, mental and spiritual well-being of a person is touched upon with this ancient technique and rightly we are celebrating it as an international event through a UN resolution since so many years and as with every year we are celebrating the international day of yoga. The theme this year being yoga for one earth, one health. As it rightly points out, it's not just about personal peace, security and happiness, but it brings in universal peace if followed by majority of the population. The beauty of yoga with my personal experience as with what I have seen in my patients that it improves not just the physical well-being but it helps to reduce the stress levels improves the immunity gets back the patients who get operated back to their normal lives quicker when followed regularly. So we have to take this day as a reminder and re-emphasize the need for each one of us. The one more the important aspect of yoga is that it can be practiced by any age with any medical background. There are ways with expert help. There are ways in which any person can start in any stage of their lives. That's the beauty of yoga and can reap the benefits of this ancient technique. So this international day of yoga, we'll again re-emphasize and realign ourselves to take a stand and start practicing yoga for our own benefit. as well as for the good of the community and for the good benefit of the planet at large. text: [Music] As the sun rises over the mountains, millions begin their day with yoga. The practice rooted in India over 5,000 years ago began as a spiritual discipline uniting the mind, body and soul. Ancient texts like the yog sutras of Patanjali laid its foundation emphasizing meditation and physical postures. In the late 19th and early 20th centuries, pioneers like Swami Viveananda introduced yoga to the west, captivating audiences with its philosophy of balance and inner peace. Fast forward to the 21st century, yoga has exploded globally, fueled by its adaptability. From hater to vinyas, yoga's physical benefits like stress relief and improved flexibility have drawn millions. By 2025, over 300 million people worldwide practice yoga with studios in every major city. Social media and celebrity endorsements have amplified its appeal, while scientific studies have validated its benefits for mental health and physical fitness. India proposed the international day of yoga which was unanimously adopted by the United Nations. Since then, millions gather annually on June 21st from New York to Nairobi celebrating yoga's unifying power. Events like mass yoga sessions and virtual classes highlight its role in promoting wellness and peace in a fast-paced world. This ancient practice born in India has stretched its way into hearts and homes across the globe. From serene studios to bustling city parks, yoga's popularity continues to soar. For centuries, yoga has been a cherished practice. Harmonizing mind, body, and soul. It is a powerful path to physical health and inner calm. It has been adopted by millions across the globe. The International Day of Yoga is a timeless tradition which celebrates yoga. So let's join in and join this tradition and this celebration of yoga. With camera person Monty from Delhi, this is Antra Sa reporting for DD India.
title: Yoga and it's Popularity | Over Years | Special | IDY 2025 | DD India

It is a spiritual discipline based on an extremely subtle science. It helps in balancing the body and mind, building inner strength and enhancing awareness. The word yoga itself means union, a connection between the individual self and the universal consciousness. The idea of dedicating a day to yoga was proposed by our prime minister Sri Narendra Modi and in 2014 the United Nations declared June 21st as international yoga day. This date was chosen because it is the summer solstice the longest day of the year in northern hemisphere symbolizing light energy and growth. [Music] Every year this day comes with a theme that encourages people to embrace yoga in different ways. This year's theme is yoga for one earth, one health. [Music] Yoga offers countless benefits. In today's world where we often find ourselves overwhelmed by stress and distraction, yoga gives us a way to reconnect with ourselves. text: Recognizing the growing need for mental well-being and inner peace in today's world, the United Nations has declared December 21st as World Meditation Day. To honor this global initiative, Buddha CEO Quantum Foundation organized meditation symposiums and awareness programs across multiple cities. These gatherings brought together meditation teachers, leaders, professionals, educators, and seekers for insightful talks on the science behind meditation, thought-provoking panel discussions, and meaningful conversations on its role in creating peace, purpose, and inner well-being. From corporate boardrooms to healthcare halls, people paused to reflect and meditate. They didn't just talk about peace. They experienced it through guided meditation sessions and the exchange of experiential wisdom. This same spirit of holistic well-being was also experienced during international yoga day celebrations with the yoga samy yoga event at pyramid valley international organized by buddha CEO quantum foundation in collaboration with the ministry of aush and ccyn government of India through Buddha CEO initiatives and partner organizations. The day was celebrated across multiple cities in India and around the world reaffirming yoga as a timeless union of body, mind and consciousness. Together these initiatives are more than just events. They are a movement toward a more conscious, healthy and compassionate world.

text: [Music] Today on the International Day of Yoga, we celebrate this ancient practice that has impacted lives across the globe. This year's theme, yoga for one earth, one health, reminds us of the deep connection between our well-being and the health of our planet. The International Day of Yoga has become a global wellness movement, uniting millions across countries. Yoga is more than just physical exercise. It is a journey towards inner peace and harmony. It teaches us to listen to our bodies and to live mindfully. As we practice yoga, we become more aware of our surroundings and more attuned with the rhythm of nature. In a world facing environmental challenges, yoga offers a path to sustainability. It encourages us to live simply, to consume mindfully, and to respect the natural world. In embracing yoga, we can reduce our ecological footprint and contribute to a healthier planet. One earth, one health reminds us that our health is not distinct and separate from the health of our environment. When we care for the earth, we care for ourselves. Clean air, pure water, and fertile soil are essential for our well-being. Yoga inspires us to protect these and ensure a thriving planet for future generations.

text: On 20th June 2025, Arunoda International Residential School proudly celebrated International Yoga Day. The event began with a traditional lamp lighting followed by a soulful prayer song by our students. Our respected principal shared the importance of yoga highlighting its power to bring balance and discipline to life. A graceful Surya Namaskar demonstration was performed by our students, showcasing the essence of physical and mental harmony. Our chief guest, Mr. Binu Anaswara, a yoga teacher and lifestyle coach, delivered an inspiring message on making yoga a part of daily life. The celebration continued with a vibrant yoga presentation by our talented students and concluded with the national anthem. The day was a true celebration of peace, wellness, and unity through yoga. Happy International Yoga Day from Arunoda International Residential School. [Music]
